Support for cairocffi (and thus those lines) were added to master the 17th January 2014 and released with version 1.4.0 (rc1 on the 13th July and several RCs later, officially released on 26th August), I see the last change to ``matplotlib_example.py`` on the 11th July 2014, so if you never tested it after then, then perhaps that explains it.  It worked then because we didn't support cffi back then but pgi magically converted it for us without any change to the mpl codebase.

My feelings on which way to use:
+ ``pgi`` using ``cairocffi`` instead of ``pycairo`` means a break in BC, i.e. if you intend for ``pgi`` to work exactly the same and interchangeably from ``pygobject``, then this does not meet that goal.  It says on the readme...

  > API compatible with PyGObject

+ That said, I also appreciate the goal of pgi to work natively with python, nice to have everything working the same way.

  > GObject Introspection bindings written in pure Python

  Ensuring we don't already have a ``cairocffi`` object before trying to convert it makes good programming sense anyway without taking ``pgi`` into consideration.

I don't think the limited use of pgi *now* makes much of a difference, instead I find the future use more pertinent.  Do you see pgi becoming stable and more usable and which way do you want to take it?  In terms of compatibility, I would expect at least a list of places where a concious decision has been made to deviate from the goal of 100% compatibility, i.e. that gtk widgets now return ``cairocffi`` contexts instead of ``cairo`` contexts.

So that's very interesting - the server is transmitting 

```
353 bot * * :Frus
```

That command is specified on page 47 in [this RFC 2812](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2812). It indicates that the first argument should be '*', '=', or '@', but the second argument should be the channel. 

Aha! Now reading [this section](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2812#section-3.2.5) of the RFC, I see that

```
At the end of this list, a list of users who are visible
but either not on any channel or not on a visible channel
are listed as being on `channel' "*".
```

But when IRC gets a channel '*', it expects it to show up in the channel list and chokes when it isn't. The bot should probably for now just ignore this value in on_namreply.

But [that issue](https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/1668), is more about using `pip install --user` instead of `sudo pip install`... both of them are bad alternatives

Installing `pew` via `pip install --user` will obviously always be supported. And I think that it's a good compromise for users to install `pipsi`,`pew`,`pipenv` with `--user`... but once such tools are in place, people should just be taught to keep everything sandboxed

Ok, this is "by design"

The idea is that you're nesting environments... This could be useful if you have developed something in B that you want to also use in A
obviously the cleaner solution would be to `pip install -e /path/to/B/`, but that way you'd include all the dependencies of B, which might not be interesting for you if B is a development tool (think something like `flake`)

Also, if you have `sayHi` installed in A as well, and you enter first B, then A like in your example, you should find that A's version is shadowing B

I'm not 100% sure this is correct, but it seems like `max_c` is 0 if the trie was just created. The loop does a single iteration, thus even if `max_c` is 0, `c` is never `0`.

```diff
diff --git a/libdatrie/datrie/darray.c b/libdatrie/datrie/darray.c
index c07712a..ea57ff3 100644
--- a/libdatrie/datrie/darray.c
+++ b/libdatrie/datrie/darray.c
@@ -774,7 +774,7 @@ da_first_separate (DArray *d, TrieIndex root, TrieString *keybuff)
                 break;
         }

-        if (c == max_c)
+        if (max_c == 0 || c == max_c)
             return TRIE_INDEX_ERROR;

         trie_string_append_char (keybuff, c);
```

The API of the `kmodes.predict` method indeed needs a `categorical` argument, but `sklearn` does not allow for extra arguments to the `predict` method. This causes `kmodes` to be incompatible with some higher-level functionality of `sklearn`, such as Pipelines.

So, this is a know issue, and can not be resolved without changes to either API.

The only solution that seems somewhat acceptable to me is to move the `categorical` argument to the `__init__` of `KModes/KPrototypes`, but I don't like it conceptually.

Yeah, that's definitely the usual way :)   Hardly any other libraries do rigorous precondition checking so they don't care, but precondition checking is hugely useful.  But that's an entirely different rant.

Anyway, here are some things to consider.  
- The usual method of having multiple .so files that use the same header files is fine for a C library but isn't going to work with a C++ template library like dlib due to the difficulty of avoiding ORD violations. 
- The existing way of compiling dlib should still work after any of these changes (also [don't complicate the build process](http://dlib.net/howto_contribute.html#9)).
- People will compile dlib on the command line without cmake.  e.g. `g++ gui_ex.cpp -ldlib` and they definitely won't remember or even know that they might have to provide a -DENABLE_ASSERTS to avoid ORD violations.  So whatever we do, a statement like `g++ your_program.cpp -ldlib` should generate a valid executable.
- Definitely things should be easy with cmake.
- #include statements of the form `#include <dlib/svm.h>` should always work.  A user should never be required to type something like `#include <dlib-debug/svm.h>` instead.  
- What if dlib is installed as an OS package (e.g. through apt-get say) and its headers are in /usr/include and a release mode dlib.so is available but a user wants to run their program in debug mode?  How do they do that? Do they download a separate copy of dlib and compile it using the current method or some other way? How are conflicts with the existing dlib (e.g. accidentally including the wrong files) made easy to avoid if this is the suggested approach?
- How does this work on windows?  Visual Studio has at least 8 different runtimes for each version of visual studio and you can't mix and match them. 

The other thing to keep in mind is that most people don't understand things like C++'s build process, how linkers work, what the ODR is or even that it exists, and so forth.  Right now the way dlib is built avoids any trouble with any of these issues at the cost of requiring each user to always compile dlib themselves.  While it would be nice to be able to say `g++ main.cpp -ldlib` and have it just work it really really needs to just work *always* no matter what.  My general philosophy is that it's better to not provide a feature at all if it can't be implemented 100% correctly.  

As for how to proceed, I'm inclined to think that the "only install a release mode library" path is reasonable. If someone wants to compile in debug mode they have to compile dlib themselves.  If a debug mode .so file were available it would be too easy for someone to do something like `g++ main.cpp -ldlib-debug` (note the missing -DENABLE_ASSERTS) and cause ORD violations.  So maybe it's setup like this: The installed dlib has its dlib/config.h setup to permanently disable asserts no matter what preprocessor directives are active.  Then when FinDlib.cmake looks for a system copy of dlib it just uses it and everything is simple.  The only question is how does someone compile dlib in debug mode?  Do they have to download a separate copy of dlib and use the current build process or is there some simpler way?  They can't use the copy in /usr/include because the config.h file there will thwart any attempt to enable asserts.  Is there some clever way to make cmake locally replace the config.h file just during a debug build by adjusting the include search path? 

Also, it's probably not a good idea to create dlib.dll files on windows.  I'm pretty sure that will only lead to user misery.  So maybe the CMake files only allow installing dlib on unix systems.  

Any FindDlib.cmake should also probably be able to automatically compile dlib if no compiled library is found so that the examples/CMakeLists.txt file can be switched to use FindDlib.cmake in a way that just works.  

What do you think?

I'm wondering if there may be some issue with releasing GPU memory from the CUDA stream context that would account for the overall memory usage climbing slowly. It's clear that the GPU memory usage climbs with the batch size, as it should, but I've found that doing a forward pass on one model with (for example) batch size 50, then doing forward passes on a second model with smaller, but increasing, batch sizes (1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50 etc), the overall GPU memory usage of the app as shown in `nvidia-smi` increases. Eventually, if I continue similar patterns, it runs out of GPU memory
```
Error while calling cudaMalloc(&data, n) in file dlib/cuda/cuda_data_ptr.cpp:28. code: 2, reason: out of memory
```

This happens even when I use `clean()` in between, so it appears that there's some situation with GPU memory management where releasing memory either isn't happening when necessary, or at all, for some portion of the used memory. *Or*, there isn't sufficient contiguous memory to reallocate on the GPU so more memory is allocated, which could also explain the slow increase.

Essentially, a single forward pass for a given model and a given batch size of images uses a particular amount of GPU memory the first time, and after some additional forward passes with the model and a second model (in the same application), that identical forward pass with the same model, batch size, and images appears to require *more* GPU memory than the first time. I can't say whether it's because memory isn't being released when it should be, or because of issues with contiguous memory allocation, or something else.

'https://clients6.google.com/chat/v1/{}?key=....'

If I understand, you're suggesting that in addition to the two existing ways of determining the top of the graph (value of the `top` parameter or the source of the first triple), I offer a third way that finds a node with in-degree 0?

Since the graphs are DAGs and not trees, there can be more than one node with in-degree 0. Consider:

```clojure
# ::snt The dog that I saw barked.
(b / bark
   :ARG0 (d / dog
            :ARG1-of (s / see
                        :ARG0 (i / i))))
```

Both `(b / bark)` and `(s / see)` have in-degree 0 (considering that `:ARG1-of` is inverted). Therefore your method is not valid as stated. What I could do is, e.g., find the *first* node with in-degree 0. We could add additional heuristics, e.g., sort nodes by those that have (a) the smallest in-degree, (b) the largest out-degree, and then choose the first one.

Maybe the most robust solution, for people with varying needs, is to let the `top` parameter take a function that chooses the top, so users can define their own criteria. E.g. redefine `top`'s setter as follows:

```python
class Graph(object):
    ...
    @top.setter
    def top(self, top):
        if callable(top):
            top = top(self._triples)
        if top not in self.variables():
            raise ValueError('top must be a valid node')
        self._top = top  # check if top is valid variable?
```

Doing this `callable()` check would slow things down when creating lots of graphs, but it might not be bad. I should benchmark it.

What do you think?

Regarding your initial issue, you can always specify the tessdata directory of your choice by passing it to the `get_languages` function or `PyTessBaseAPI` init (and pretty much every other helper function). Example:

```python
tesserocr.get_languages('C:\\Dev\\Anaconda\\envs\\fmtk\\tessdata/')
api = tesserocr.PyTessBaseAPI(path='C:\\Dev\\Anaconda\\envs\\fmtk\\tessdata/')
```

As for your updated question, you can create a config file and load it using the `ReadConfigFile` method:

```python
with tesserocr.PyTessBaseAPI(psm=tesserocr.PSM.SINGLE_LINE) as api:
    api.ReadConfigFile('C:\\path\\to\\config')
    # use api as usual...
```
You can check examples of config files [here](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/tree/master/tessdata/tessconfigs). Hope this helps.

*Extra tip*: No need to call `api.Init` after initializing the api object since it calls it automatically with the given parameters passed while initializing the api instance. Only call `Init` if you wish to re-initialize with different parameters.

In the FIX protocol, each field has a tag and a value, separated by '=' and terminated by SOH.  *Except* for "data" fields.

Data fields are used to contain values that might need to include an SOH value: things like UTF-8 strings, or some other binary data.  They work by using a pair of fields: the first must encode the length of the data for the second.  When parsing the second field, instead of scanning ahead for the SOH, you use the length specified in the previous field.  There should still be an SOH at the end, but by using the length you can skip over any embedded SOH bytes in the middle.

So, in *theory*, field tag 96 is RawData, and it must be preceded by field 95, RawDataLen.  But ... as you have seen, the theory and the practice are not always consistent in how FIX is actually implemented.

simplefix has a list of the defined data field tags, and their matching length field tags.  Where it's getting confused is that it's seeing 96 without a preceding 95.

So ...

I've got a patch that will fix this: unless there's a `raw_len > 0` (ie. we've seen the length tag already), the parser will now treat 95 (and any other raw data fields) like a normal field.  It's not quite legit according to the letter of the spec, but I think it'll work out ok in practice.

If you registered two classes for the same thing, the parser cannot know which one to use when receiving a stanza. There must only ever be a single class for each TAG registered on an XML stream object.

To avoid this, you have two options:

* change the TAG in your subclass (only viable if you’re inventing a new protocol; rarely the right thing to do)
* monkey patch the existing class. This is the preferred and supported way for extending XSOs.

To avoid name collisions, you should prefix your monkey-patched attributes. E.g.:

```python
aioxmpp.httpupload.xso.Request.xep1234_foo = aioxmpp.xso.…
```

If your extension is proprietary, prefix it with your domain name or something.

Hi, I've logged #84 to request this exact feature. 

Raising the possibility of write failure is fair, so that if writing to a file is implemented, we should make sure a non-zero exit code is return by acme-tiny upon write failure. Need to look closer at this change to ensure it achieves this. 

But I'll argue that feature would provide a compromise compare to what the documentation told you to do now:

The docs tell users to redirect directly to your desired output file, which will overwrite an existing certs in case any kind of of error occur: 
`python acme_tiny.py --account-key ./account.key --csr ./domain.csr --acme-dir /usr/share/nginx/html/.well-known/acme-challenge/ > signed.crt`

On the other hand, implementing the output options would allow acme-tiny to abort before writing the file in case there's an error during the signing. It could also report an error if the writing fails for any reason, so it a worst case (partially truncated files), the user would be warned. 

I don't think you can have safe writing, even when using temporary files. The temporary folder could be r+w while the final output could be ro. Even if access rights are okay, filesystem relies on physical medium (sometime even rotating/mechanical ones), point is you there are problem you can't predict, but you an report errors.

@terrycain Thanks very much for taking the time and energy to figure this out. To be 100% honest, I am just returning from some time away, so I have not thoroughly reviewed the gist or the RFC. I will. But, nonetheless, here are my initial impressions.

1. Yes, a PR like this is definitely welcome.
1. To the extent possible, I would like to minimize the number of dependencies. Therefore, perhaps this would not be included by default, but would require a separate dependency `pip install sanic-jwt[something]`.
1. Another alternative, which was more along the lines of my initial thinking, the getting of the secret would be a handler that could easily be overridden. By default, it would just be an `async` method that is passed (for example) the `request`  object and returns the secret from the configurations. Then ... the user could override this with any custom method, including one that would go out and make another API call.
1. I have not specifically tested this, but using the new `get_<setting>()` methods from v1.1 ([see here](http://sanic-jwt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/configuration.html#configuration-class)), this should be possible.

```python
class MyConfig(Configuration):

    def get_secret(self, request):
        return "some secret"

Initialize(
    app,
    configuration_class=MyConfig
```

#### before idf.run()

Jamie,

Some thoughts on organizing idf.run()
- Let us take the example of IDF.getobject()
- You have worked on this function, so you are familiar with it.
- Notice that there is a plain vanilla function getobject()
- The method IDF.getobject() calls getobject()
- I prefer writing in this style, where there is a function that can be called directly.
- If appropriate, this function can then be called from any class
- My sense is that, this gives more flexibility as the code evolves
- It also allows you to run an idf file without using the class IDF

So when you write the run() function, it might look like this:

    import eppy.runner.run_functions as run_functions
    # since your code in the runner folder
    # 
    run_functions.run()
    # 
    # don't be tied to any of the names I have used
    # - runner, run_functions, or run.
    # if there is a more intuitive naming strategy, go for it

If you have other thoughts on this, let me know.

@mgeier A priori, I would imagine that seeking is approximately free for uncompressed files, but expensive for compressed files. Regardless, I would think that shuffling some numpy memory should always be a bit less expensive than calling out to a C library and updating file pointers.

Let's see how my intuition compares to reality ;-)

Current Master:

```python
>>> %timeit for block in soundfile.blocks('still_alive.wav', 512, 0): pass
773 ms ± 3.53 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

>>> %timeit for block in soundfile.blocks('still_alive.wav', 512, 256): pass
1.58 s ± 13.4 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

>>> %timeit for block in soundfile.blocks('still_alive.flac', 512, 0): pass
9.51 s ± 83 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

>>> %timeit for block in soundfile.blocks('still_alive.flac', 512, 256): pass
36.1 s ± 422 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
```

Your branch:

```python
>>> %timeit for block in soundfile.blocks('still_alive.wav', 512, 0): pass
622 ms ± 22.4 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

>>> %timeit for block in soundfile.blocks('still_alive.wav', 512, 256): pass
1.21 s ± 4.69 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

>>> %timeit for block in soundfile.blocks('still_alive.flac', 512, 0): pass
9.13 s ± 128 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

>>> %timeit for block in soundfile.blocks('still_alive.flac', 512, 256): pass
18.3 s ± 47.4 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
```

As you can see, there is a modest performance benefit for non-compressed files or zero overlap (5%-30%), but a substantial (2x) benefit for compressed files with nonzero overlap! 

This performance benefit, and getting rid of the `seek` for nonseekable files, makes this very worthwhile in my eyes. Do you have any objections to merging this, @mgeier?

- - -

As a side note, I would not have thought that block processing takes so much time! Simply reading the whole file takes

```python
>>> %timeit soundfile.read('still_alive.wav');
70.2 ms ± 258 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)

>>> %timeit soundfile.read('still_alive.flac');
235 ms ± 3.97 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
```

while reading a single block takes

```python
>>> %timeit soundfile.read('still_alive.wav', start=44100, frames=512);
160 µs ± 230 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)

>>> %timeit soundfile.read('still_alive.flac', start=44100, frames=512);
811 µs ± 2.64 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
```

which this adds up to the 18-36 s of processing time if you multiply it with 30k blocks. 

It might be worthwhile for `blocks` to read larger chunks and split them into blocks internally. Is anyone interested in trying this?

`natto-py` honors nearly the exact same options as when you use `mecab` from the command-line.

So the following two approaches are equivalent:

    # mecab from command-line
    mecab --dicdir=/usr/local/lib/mecab/dic/mecab-ko-dic

    # with natto-py
    nm = MeCab('--dicdir=/usr/local/lib/mecab/dic/mecab-ko-dic')

It is a good idea to try out your choice of options first at the `mecab` command-line before using them in instantiating `MeCab()` with `natto-py`.

Hope that helps!

Part of the reason I didn't implement range intersection is that it was ambiguous what should happen if intervals within one or both of the input trees overlapped each other:

* Should the intervals be intersected first?
* If so, should those initial intersections *within* the input trees handle the `data` field differently from the intersections *between* the input trees?
* Should the product tree mark each intersection of the `N` intervals in tree A with the `M` intervals of tree B to produce up to `N*M` result intervals in the result tree?

Rather than impose my idea of what to do in these cases, I decided to leave this up to users to implement.

Here's a basic algorithm, which you can test and modify. I'm having some issues with the automated tests in Travis, so I can't easily add this method at the moment, but I hope this will get you unstuck.

```python
def complement(tree):
    result = IntervalTree()
    result.addi(tree.begin(), tree.end())  # using input tree bounds
    for iv in tree:
        result.chop(iv)
    return result
```

I installed opencv 3.3.1 from sources, but I did copied then so file to wrong folder. 
```
sudo cp ~/opencv/build/lib/python3/cv2.cpython-34m.so /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/
```
as it was suggested in tutorial.

But in fact there were 2 versions of so files:
```
3.3.0 => /usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/cv2/cv2.cpython-34m.so 
```
and 
```
3.3.1 => /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/cv2.cpython-34m.so
```
So looks like it should be in `cv2` folder

And I did this (made backup first, and then replaced 3.3.0 file to lib64... cv2/ by new file 3.3.1):
```
sudo cp ~/opencv/build/lib/python3/cv2.cpython-34m.so /usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/cv2/
```

Ah, now I get it. Sorry I was inattentive.

You are correct, the 

```python
myproject.wiki_pages.get(u'PageName', version=17)
```
won't work, because in this case ``wiki_pages`` is a ResourceSet object of the Project Resource object and it's ``get`` method has no idea about the version keyword argument, because it's just a helper to get the needed Resource from a ResourceSet by id. So one way of solving the problem is like this:

```python
for wiki_page in myproject.wiki_pages:
    version = wiki_page.refresh(version=17)
    # do something with the version
```

``refresh`` method is available on all resources and is very useful when you need to re-request something from Redmine which is not available by default (like another version of the wiki page).

The other way you already know, as stated in the docs it is:

```python
redmine.wiki_page.get('PageName', project_id='myProjectId', version=12)
```

In this case you are calling the ``get`` method on the ResourceManager object which requests data directly from Redmine and that is why that works.

Hope that helps.

It's a valid question. We've implemented the BG/NBD model by incrementing tenure by +1 so that tenure > recency always, and we seem to be able to use first-time buyers without a problem. Their value depends heavily upon the aggregate, but we have the wonderful good fortune that our mean spend per visit skews left enough that it strongly resembles a Gamma function.

That said, deviating from a theoretical basis always has to be done carefully. Customer acquisition is distinct from customer loyalty as a business question, and in marketing research has typically been modeled separately, both because of the nature of the problem (it's technically impossible to prove cross-sell to a first-time customer, and recommender systems can not be based on history) and because of the cost associated with it.

That said, using first-time customers in the context of the double-Gamma model may produce useful results, even if it isn't designed to model them.

Correct, the inputs don't have nodes associated with them. I need to document this, but the internal term I use for inputs and outputs is "pins" which are not neurons.  This is originally because having full nodes for inputs meant you had a bunch of useless machinery in every input neuron that didn't do much of anything.  (I sometimes still wonder if that was a good idea...but I haven't talked myself into changing it back yet.)

Each input pin is labeled with a negative integer, starting at -1.  Each output pin always has an associated node (which can't be deleted by mutation), and the output pins/neurons are always numbered starting at 0.  So in your example, 0 is the output node/pin, and 1 is a hidden node that is connected to inputs 1 and 3.

I don't understand what's going under the hood, but here is what I have found.

When `DEBUG` is True, the django.contrib.admin app will apply some check functions on the ModelAdmin class.

When executing the `_check_list_display` on `CommentsAdmin`, this provoke the issue when checking the attribute `'name'` which is a property, not a real field. 

It seems that the check function make a call on` model._meta.get_field(item)`, and destroy the the relationship between the models for a non obvious reason, but certainly related on how the property is calculated (it's a DB call)... 

Making this check call later solve the issue, because the relationship on the Entry model seems OK.

Django_comment should change the CommentsAdmin.list_display to make 'name' a callable, which will defer the check, and do it properly, but I don't think that they will change this now...

So I will the update the documentation.

So this is still an issue, and I think it's all about how _optimize combines operations like remove and add as replace and move operations.
```{'op' : 'remove', 'path' : '/0', 'value' : 'some matched value'}```
``` ... ```
```{'op' : 'add', 'path' : '/1', 'value' : 'some matched value'}```
At first it may seem logical to replace these two commands with a single move command, but in order to do that properly you have to consider all the commands that alter the state of this array in between.

Let's say you replace the first command with a 'move' and delete the add:
```{'op' : 'move', 'from' : '/0', 'path' : '/1'}```
``` ... ```
``` <deleted> ```
But that path '/1' could actually be wrong. Any add or remove operations that take place between these two will alter what the correct location is supposed to be at the time of the move.

I ran into this today, but in connection to null object values. My workaround was to monkey patch bravado_core like so:
~~~
# Monkey patch bravado to work around null object values
import bravado_core.unmarshal
old_unmarshal_object = bravado_core.unmarshal.unmarshal_object


def _unmarshal_object(swagger_spec, object_spec, object_value):
    if object_value is None:
        return None
    return old_unmarshal_object(swagger_spec, object_spec, object_value)

bravado_core.unmarshal.unmarshal_object = _unmarshal_object
# End monkey patch
~~~

pyzmq uses distutils' `ccompiler.has_function` to check for `timer_create`. distutils, in turn, uses the stdlib tempfile module to create a temporary c file to compile and perform the check. I used this method (altered from Natims code):
`def upload_directory(src_dir, bucket_name, dst_dir):
    if not os.path.isdir(src_dir):
        raise ValueError('src_dir %r not found.' % src_dir)

    all_files = []
    for root, dirs, files in os.walk(src_dir):
        all_files += [os.path.join(root, f) for f in files]

    s3_resource = boto3.resource('s3')
    for filename in all_files:
        s3_resource.Object(bucket_name, os.path.join(dst_dir, os.path.relpath(filename, src_dir)))\
            .put(Body=open(filename, 'rb'))`

The main differences (other then logging and different checks) is that this method copies all files in the directory recursively, and that it allows changing the root path in s3 (inside the bucket).

Great - if you have access to repositories with plenty of binary data in it, you should be able to test for the error using [this test](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/gitdb/blob/master/gitdb/test/performance/test_pack.py#L88).

The `self.gitrepopath` symbol is initialized with the value of the `GITDB_TEST_GIT_REPO_BASE` environment variable, which you can just set to the `.git` directory of the repository you want to test all files in.

The memory database will do exactly what a loose object database does, except that it only writes to and reads from memory, and I have just verified it truly spots the issue with the file you provided with the previous patch removed.

## ;tldr

To test your repository in its entirety, set an environment variable to the repository and run a particular test using nose.
```bash
 export GITDB_TEST_GIT_REPO_BASE=myrepo/.git
 nosetests gitdb/test/performance/test_pack.py -m test_loose_correctness
```

Some thoughts:

*  `__PYVENV_LAUNCHER__` was added because for earlier versions of Python (< 3.3, according to Ronald) but not for later versions, Python's `site.py` on OS X needed to distinguish the location of stub launchers from framework executables. According to [this comment](https://bugs.python.org/issue22490#msg229119) by Ned Deily, `sys.executable` now points to the stub launcher, and this is why `distlib` stopped using `__PYVENV_LAUNCHER__`. The only usage of it now is in `site.py` - if available, on OS X, it's used to locate the `pyvenv.cfg` file.

* It may be worth checking to see what happens in e.g. Python 3.4, to see if's some sort of regression, or whether all Python >= 3.3 has the same behaviour.

* My guess is that something, somewhere, is doing an `os.path.realpath()` call when it shouldn't be. It doesn't look like it's `distlib` doing that. If (as per Ned's comment) `sys.executable` and the env var are always the same, then it shouldn't matter; but if something does a `realpath` on a `sys.executable` and executes Python through the result, then that would result in an unexpected shebang (because `sys.executable` would then be a dereferenced path).

Addressing issue #206 requires 2 things to be complete:

@kevin-keraudren - I'm afraid test 11 is going to fail unless we were to push the operations to set the pixels onto the priority queue (currently marker 1 sets all of the pixels of its neighbors, then marker 2 sets all of the pixels of its neighbors except for the ones set by marker 1). The algorithm could work like this:
* push all of the seeds on the queue
* loop (exit when queue is empty):
  * pop a location off of the queue
  * if the location is unmarked:
    * propagate the label to the location
    * push all unmarked neighbors onto queue with appropriate ages.

This uses more memory (can push the same destination location from several similarly-labeled pixels along the propagation front, requires storing the label in the priority queue) and is only marginally more accurate. I'm not sure if it's worth the added effort.
 
@jni - if you rely on the propagation age as I did, as opposed to the incremental age, a marker in the middle of a big basin will meet the edge of a plateau with an age that's larger than a marker in the middle of a small basin, so you need to reset as you enter a plateau. You know you're propagating across a plateau when the image value of the source pixel equals that of the destination pixel.
The ordering of the structuring element is based on the manhattan distance - the sum of the absolute values of the offsets. In 2-d, a diagonal has a value of 2 and horizontal/vertical have a value of 1. In 3-d, there's an additional diagonal to the corner which has a value of 3. I subtract 1 because the minimum distance is 1. I'm assuming nobody is going to use a structuring element that's more than 3 on a side... if that assumption is wrong, then you'd need to compute the euclidean distance, rank-order the unique values and assign the rank-order to the structuring elements. I had considered ordering the structuring elements by distance and then Kevin's age strategy might work if the horizontal/vertical elements entered the queue ahead of the diagonal ones.

Done with second round of review. I think that covers all of my feedback.

* In determining how to let the user choose what OPF file to use, all of those sound like good suggestions. It depends mostly on how users typically use the OPF, and about this I am somewhat ignorant. The most universally easy solution would be to launch a file browser from the web UI. We could also allow users to put in a path manually, which could be a publicly accessible URL. So, maybe there would be a "path" field, where the user types in the path, and a button for launching a file system browser for finding and loading local files.
* For parsing OPF files, would it be OK to hard-code into our script exceptions for `anomalyScore`, `multiStepBestPredictions.1`, and `multiStepBestPredictions.5` and the like? That would make it pretty easy, as long as we can count on those fields always being number-like (we can use type coercion to turn them into numbers). It makes things more tightly coupled, but so far, this would only be for OPF, so that should be OK, right?
* For smooth zooming, it don't see an option for that in DyGraphs, but I will look around, and I may be able to add a plugin for that as well.
* Will worry about the anomalyAnnotation later. I think what I will end up having is two different versions of this script - though working in the same way - one for NAB, and one for OPF. Alternatively, we could perhaps use one script, but give it a config somehow, so it behaves slightly differently depending on if it is working with OPF data, or NAB data.

Be aware that you can't substract timezone aware datetime and timezone naive datetime
So it may fail if `self.start` is a timezone aware datetime (because your epoch is `datetime.datetime(1970, 1, 1)` which is timezone naive datetime)

EPOCH should in fact be `datetime.datetime(1970, 1, 1, tzinfo=pytz.UTC)`

so we may wonder what will happen if `self.start` is timezone aware but not UTC?

What ever change will be... we should have `_sanitize_dates` function in mind
https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/blob/master/pandas_datareader/_utils.py#L18

Calculating difference between 2 datetime with differents timezone (according DST or not ...) may be a bit tricky (but some Python library can do this)

With Python 3 and Pandas 0.20 there is a very convenient method:

```python
pd.Timestamp('1970-01-01', tz='UTC').timestamp()
```

unfortunately not with Python 2.7 and same Pandas version

It needs to be added here:

https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/blob/master/.travis.yml#L54

to be available on TravisCI.

@HereticSK I understand your point, though it's quite safe to assume that the forward returns computed with one factor applies to other factors, which allow to re-use forward returns. As long as multiple factors have the same date index the forward returns will be the same, e.g. multiple factors computed daily at market open. 

I don't believe it would be easy to remove `factor` from `compute_forward_returns` arguments, there are many scenarios covered by Alphalens where your proposed solution wouldn't work. Have a look at intraday NB example, where the prices are those:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15273575/36346395-188e78a4-143e-11e8-9b89-e4f5a3018b58.png)

Given those prices and `periods=(1, 2, 3, 6)` the function `compute_forward_returns` has to infer that the actual time periods are 1h, 3h, 1D, 2D. Without having the factor date index, which gives the information of when the trades happen, I don't believe the function can compute the required output.

Regarding the upserts, I think I can capture the execution_id when the data is loaded through the API to Domo. I am typically loading data from the previous day, so each execution ID represents a day of data, but maybe in a month from now I will need to replace that data with a fresher version. (A common example is repeat data - a case might not have a repeat today, but within the next 30 days the customer might call back and that row of data will be stale in Domo)

So create a simple table like this:
```
date, execution_id
2017-10-11, 674
2017-10-12, 675
```

Then, if I need to add 2017-10-11 data again, I can lookup any previous execution_ids, delete them, and load a fresh version to Domo without having to reload the whole dataset or creating duplicates. Any issues with this approach? 

The other option is to use the 'insert_timestamp' which gets updated each time there is a change. All new rows would be appended to the Domo dataset but only the latest version would be used (perhaps as simple as a select distinct operation, ordering by the insert_timestamp). Seems somewhat wasteful though, especially for large datasets.

Je pense que cette PR est symptomatique des difficultés de contribution, avec :
- les contraintes de la vectorisation
- les répétitions dues à la séparation règles / parmamètres
- la gestion des évolutions temporelle de la legislation (à moitié dans les params, à moitié dans les règles sous différentes formes)

Evidemment la lisibilité de la loi en souffre au moins autant. 

D'où mon orientation vers la définition d'un format de : 
-  _données_ (pas de code, chacun est libre de développer son moteur à execution vectorisée performante, distribuée, ou dans le navigateur par exemple)   
- basé sur des templates de calcul _spécialisés par domaine_ (dans un premier temps au moins) qui permettent plus d'expressivité en joignant règles et params
- qui traite la période de simulation comme une variable du système (le format de conditions 2004-01-01: 0.4%, 2003-01-01: 0.5% etc. étant simplement un template de calcul parmi d'autres)

(voilà un [format possible](https://github.com/laem/syso/blob/master/r%C3%A8gles/r%C3%A9mun%C3%A9ration-travail/cotisations/taxe-apprentissage.yaml#L32) pour la cotisation ciblée par cette PR. C'est un brouillon non-exécuté)

Read: 1970-01-01T00:00:00+00:00

```
import datetime
import pytz

from gcloud import bigtable
instance_id = 'bigtabletesting'
table_id = 'Hello-Bigtable'

client = bigtable.Client(admin=True)
instance = client.instance(instance_id, 'us-central1-c')
table = instance.table(table_id)
client.start()

column_family_id = 'cf1'
cf1 = table.column_family(column_family_id)
table.create(column_families=[cf1])

timestamp = datetime.datetime(1970, 1, 1, microsecond=200, tzinfo=pytz.utc)
row = table.row(b'test')
row.set_cell(
        column_family_id,
            b'greeting', b'Hello World!',
                timestamp=timestamp)
row.commit()

partial_data = table.read_row(row._row_key)
assert(len(partial_data.cells["cf1"]["greeting"]) == 1)
# These should be equal, but they are not because microseconds are lost.
print "Read: %s" % partial_data.cells["cf1"]["greeting"][0].timestamp.isoformat()
print "Set: %s" % timestamp.isoformat()
```

The most important use of this class is that there are places in the returned API resources where we just get a reference to a table or dataset. Rather than have a Table or Dataset class with no properties besides the ID filled in, I propose using reference classes to be more clear that they are just pointers. This would prevent confusion such as https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/3754

The reference classes would also be used as the argument to new methods such as `client.get_dataset(dataset_ref)` and `client.get_table(table_ref)`.

we can't support `-m` self-referentially in a built pex, because the pex may take it's own arguments that this would interfere with. however, this option could easily be plumbed in a built pex by the author as needed.

it's worth noting that pex does support this mode of execution today via environment variables - specifically, `PEX_MODULE` - which executes module execution (`some.mymodule`) as well as importable entrypoints (`some.module:main`) - and `PEX_SCRIPT` which executes console script entrypoints.

```
[omerta ~]$ pex pip -o /tmp/throwaway.pex
[omerta ~]$ /tmp/throwaway.pex
Python 2.7.10 (default, Dec 16 2015, 14:09:45)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.0.2 (clang-700.1.81)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
(InteractiveConsole)
>>>
[omerta ~]$ PEX_SCRIPT=pip /tmp/throwaway.pex

Usage:
  pip <command> [options]

Commands:
  install                     Install packages.
...
[omerta ~]$ PEX_MODULE='pip' /tmp/throwaway.pex

Usage:
  pip <command> [options]

Commands:
  install                     Install packages.
...
[omerta ~]$ echo '{"test": 1}' | PEX_MODULE="json.tool" /tmp/throwaway.pex
{
    "test": 1
}
```

so, you could achieve this today by plumbing `-m` in your pex main or shim to re-exec itself with one of these environment variables defined to net this effect.

"exabgp_log_all=true" is just a way of setting an environment variable before starting the exabgp process itself. you can find about environment variables by just starting exabgp with no argument (see option 4) to set env. variables below).

```
ExaBGP will automatically look for its configuration file (in windows ini format):
 * in the etc/exabgp folder located within the extracted tar.gz
 * in /etc/exabgp/exabgp.env

Individual configuration options can be set using environment variables, such as:
 > env exabgp.daemon.daemonize=true ./sbin/exabgp
or:
 > env exabgp.daemon.daemonize=true ./sbin/exabgp
or:
 > export exabgp.daemon.daemonize=true; ./sbin/exabgp

Multiple environment values can be set, the order of preference being:
 1) command line environment value using dot separated notation
 2) exported value from the shell using dot separated notation
 3) command line environment value using underscore separated notation
 4) exported value from the shell using underscore separated notation
 5) the value in the ini configuration file
 6) the built-in defaults
```

I don't know about the specifics of the FreeBSD packaging (I'm using Linux), but you should probably stop the stock ExaBGP daemon first (using the rc.d script, something like "/etc/rc.d/exabgp stop") and then try:

exabgp_daemon_daemonize=false exabgp_log_all=true exabgp <your_conf_file>

(to make sure ExaBGP does not daemonize itself). As for your trace above, I don't see any "routes" messages showing routes updates from the Cisco router. Try adding "exabgp_log_routes=true" just in case (but "exabgp_log_all=true" should already take care of this).

The general approach looks good to me.  I just have three questions:

1. We still need some way of telling `Embedding` or any other similar class that we're loading for training vs. loading for test.  We currently do that by removing the key for the embedding file, but if we want to be able to expand vocab, we can't anymore, if we want to actually use the embedding file at test time to expand our vocab.  One option here is instead of (or in addition to) passing a mapping from hocon keys to filenames, have a function that modifies the `Params` object on load.  You currently have that in `load_archive`, but I wonder if we can pass that logic on to the `FileArchiver` somehow, so that `Embedding` can do more than just replace a filename.  Seems a little tricky...

Actually, taking a step back on this point...  The only reason we remove the filename key for the `Embedding` is because we might not have access to the original file, and we don't need it anyway, because the weights are saved in the model.  If we _did_ always have the file, we'd just be spending a few seconds loading the embeddings before replacing them with the loaded model state.  So, removing the key from the parameters isn't actually necessary, it just might save a few seconds on loading.

This is a long way of saying that I originally thought there was something missing here, but, now that I think about it a bit more, this will work fine as you have it for `Embedding`.  We'll just need to implement some "expand vocab" functionality now that we're guaranteed to have access to the embedding file at test time.

2. Can `FileArchivers` clobber each other in the archive?  It looks like they currently can, if two different archivers pick the same key.

3. Is there an easy way to avoid doing this with multiple inheritance?  The class-level `_param_history` made me cringe a little - you're doing that so mypy is happy, because you can't have a constructor, because you can't construct it due to the multiple inheritance issue.  If this is the best solution, that's fine; I can't think of a better way to do this right now, either.

 - if a user is not authenticated, the `activated` variable should be unknown or None, and not activated False that implies it's not activated (for example, all three of the endpoints above *were* active but returned as activated False in the initial queries.   - activated but not accessible (cue the link you showed me above)
  - not activated and not accessible (I wouldn't give them any link here, saving a lot of clicks)
  - not activated and accessible (this would be where contacted the cluster admin is relevant).

To record here a comment that arose elsewhere:

The questions raised in this issue are not so much about how to change
the files. Rather, they are requests to the copyright holders, to
clarify explicitly what is the grant of license.

The question is for the copyright holders to answer: What set of
license conditions do you, the copyright holders, explicitly grant to
recipients of this work?

A grant of license is some explicit text, from the copyright holders
of the work, that says something like “you, the recipient of this
work, are free to do x, y, and z, under [some specific set of
conditions]”.

The specific set of conditions can't be guessed reliably. It might be
“the GNU GPL version 2 or later”, or might be “the GNU GPL version 3
only”, or might be “the GNU GPL version 3 or later or the Apache
License version 2 or later”; or, well, any weird set of conditions, in
my experience.

This is one reason why the GNU GPL has useful instructions “How to
Apply These Terms to Your New Programs”. But again, the “you” there
can only be the copyright holder; we can't make those statements on
their behalf.

Without that, we aren't in a position to guess the intent of the
copyright holders.

Indeed. I should make one or both of those fixes and push a new version of pyDelphin. I haven't given this repo much attention for the last few months.

> Either way, I think it might make sense to check for DMRS compatibility before doing the conversion

Yes, but a full check would make things rather slow; and could be annoying if you are running a batch process to convert many MRSs and pyDelphin quits in the middle of it. Instead, users who care about the cleanliness of their representations can manually check an Xmrs with [`Xmrs.is_well_formed()`](https://github.com/delph-in/pydelphin/blob/7a2136f303ac3a51b01b88645e9ee42bd58970eb/delphin/mrs/xmrs.py#L476) (assuming that a well-formed Xmrs is compatible with DMRS)

> for any variable, there should be a unique predicate taking it as ARG0 (except for quantifiers). 

I would say "for any `x` or `e` variable" since `i`, `p`, `u`, and `h` variables don't need to be or can't be ARG0s. But there's some disagreement about whether we can have an `x` or `e` without the predication for which it is an ARG0, and I've also seen `i` variables (and maybe even `u`) as ARG0s for some predications. Where my intuitive understanding of *MRS was at odds with *MRSs I observed in the wild, I opted for pyDelphin to be more robust and attempt to model (what I thought was) ill-formed *MRSs.

> Throwing an error (or at least a warning) if this is not the case would be a very helpful indication that something's going wrong.

In some cases I do throw errors ([example](https://github.com/delph-in/pydelphin/blob/7a2136f303ac3a51b01b88645e9ee42bd58970eb/delphin/mrs/xmrs.py#L108)), and at one point I threw warnings or log messages, but yes, I should be more consistent about this. The rule of thumb is: raise an exception if the problem is unrecoverable, warn if it might give unexpected results but can otherwise safely be ignored, and be quiet for things that are simply surprising but don't cause problems.

@ihaque-freenome Thanks for the pass and fail cases, very useful.  See pull request samtools/htslib#653.  I'll need @jkbonfield to take a look over it before merging, as he's the expert in this area.

In both your tests, it looks like the requested region was after the last read on the chromosome, so the correct result is to return no reads.  In both cases the index caused reading to start at the file location just after all of the chr8 data.  What's then supposed to happen is that `cram_next_slice()` reads a container header, discovers it is for a location beyond the requested range and so sets `fd->eof = 1` and exits.

For the failure case (HG00114.0025.cram) what actually happened was that it skipped over lots of containers due to a missing check at [cram_decode.c line 2944](https://github.com/samtools/htslib/blob/1c5c5089/cram/cram_decode.c#L2944) until it hit one for chromosome X.  That had an end position high enough to terminate the loop.  The check at [line 2954](https://github.com/samtools/htslib/blob/1c5c5089/cram/cram_decode.c#L2954) then caused the function to exit, but without setting the `eof` flag.  This was then interpreted as an error.

For the pass case (HG00114.0005.cram) the same container skipping process happened, but for that one it ended when it reached a container with reads for more than one reference in it.  These use the special reference id -2, which allowed it to pass the test at [line 2954](https://github.com/samtools/htslib/blob/1c5c5089/cram/cram_decode.c#L2954).  The end of the region is then detected much later (probably around [line 3200](https://github.com/samtools/htslib/blob/1c5c5089/cram/cram_decode.c#L3200)) and in this case correctly flagged by setting `fd->eof = 1`.

It really depends on what you mean by "spawning the notebook application". If you want to launch a notebook with the full UI, then navigating to `[base_url]/notebooks/[notebook-name.ipynb]` (e.g. in an IFrame) is everything. You don't need to make any API requests for that. If you want to launch kernels via the API and write your own notebook application, and want to spawn a kernel for a given notebook, then you make a POST request to `/api/sessions`, as described [in the REST API](http://petstore.swagger.io/?url=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jupyter/notebook/4.2.2/notebook/services/api/api.yaml#!/sessions/post_sessions).

> @JoelBondurant <https://github.com/JoelBondurant> If you are presenting
> code and don't need to actually execute it (which is presumably what you
> would be doing if you're hiding the code) you can use nbconvert.
>
> How exactly you would use it would depend on what exactly you want.
>
> For example, if you wanted to explore a notebook as though you were going
> through the notebook but without having to see or execute any of the code,
> you could export to html and use something like @parente
> <https://github.com/parente>'s solution to hide all of the input cells
> (as a PR jupyter/nbviewer#681
> <https://github.com/jupyter/nbviewer/pull/681> and as a blogpost
> <http://mindtrove.info/code-hiding-on-nbviewer/>.
>
> If you want a bit more flexibility (e.g., showing some inputs, but not
> all; or being able to toggle the visibility of individual cells' input),
> you could use the approach I took in https://github.com/mpacer/
> hiding_tags_nbconvert. In that case, you would tag all the cells that for
> which the input would be eliminated with kill_input (and you would use
> the kill_hidden.tpl template). That would solve your problem in the same
> way as above, but on a per cell basis.
>
> However, suppose someone asked a question specifically about the code, you
> might want to make it accessible; that wouldn't be possible if the cells
> were actually not present(which is what the kill_hidden.tpl does… thus
> the ominous moniker of kill). However, you might want to only apply this
> to some cells, and you might not want to have to toggle all the cells at
> once in order to show just one (which may cause scrolling issues for that
> one cell). Then you want the toggle_hidden.tpl template, which creates
> per-cell visibility that is controlled by a toggle control (e.g., see the
> checkbox next to {%- block any_cell -%} at this example site
> <https://mpacer.github.io/hiding_tags_nbconvert/hide_cells_based_on_tags.html>
> for to see what I mean). In that case, you would tag code cells with
> hide_input and would use the toggle_hidden.tpl.
>
> If you wanted to have a blending of the two, that's just a matter of me
> writing a new template which wouldn't be hard, but won't a priority for me
> unless someone specifically asks for it.

Ugh, I hate that YAML silently drops duplicate lines. There's a [very hacky warning](https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/blob/master/peru/parser.py#L209) in place to try to detect cases where that's happening though. Did it trigger for you when you duplicated the `delete` field?

As happy as I am to see someone using a multi-rule pipe, you're right that you don't need it in this case. What you need is to give `delete` a YAML list of entries. So either this:

```yaml
rule skip-default-wp-themes:
  delete:
    - wp-content/themes/twenty*
    - wp-content/plugins/hello.php
```

or this

```yaml
rule skip-default-wp-themes:
  delete: ["wp-content/themes/twenty*", "wp-content/plugins/hello.php"]
```

I don't think the quotes are strictly necessary in that second example, but YAML parsing starts to get really dicey as soon as `*` and other interesting syntax gets involved.

You mean splitting out different appliance config sections into their own individual config files? We certainly could go that route. I haven't fully thought through it yet though.

It would require reworking how the client handles config loading. Right now the client just expects the name of the config file section it should load settings from. We could change that to be a config file path. Or we could add a separate mechanism for specifying the location of the config file.

I actually have a partial patch now that improves how we load config settings, supporting multiple possible config file locations. Right now we just read from the config file included in the PyKMIP distribution; that's bad and really should only be used as a last resort option. Instead, we want to be able to read config from standard locations, like the user's home directory, ~/.config, or /etc. Placing appliance sensitive config information in config files in these locations would allow you to avoid mixing sensitive data with the repo.

I'm ok with exploring this route; however my short term goal here is to make sure that everything's in place by the end of this week for the integration test work. I don't want a rework of the config handling to block that.

Hi @mingyr 

There are a few bugs in the error message here, apologies for the confusion. This will be fixed in the next version. The corrected error message should say:

```
ValueError: The outputs of the provided cores are not able to be concatenated along the first feature dimension. Core 0 has shape [32, 32, 4], whereas Core 1 has shape [32, 32, 8] - these must only differ in the first dimension
```

You've selected skip connections in DeepRNN so it's trying to concatenate the outputs from the two cores together to provide the final output. The sizes shown are the sizes without batch dimension, and the implementation concatenates on the first non-batch dimension. Therefore the rest of the shapes (in this case [32, 4] and [32, 8]) need to be the same, but they aren't.

If you disable skip connections this won't be a problem. If you definitely want skip connections, then presumably you are looking to concatenate on the channels dimension? You could either modify DeepRNN so that the concatenation dimension is customisable, or you could transpose your data into NCHW format, meaning you would have [4, 32, 32] and [8, 32, 32] which can be concatenated. Obviously this will require changes elsewhere too.

I hope this helps! Please let me know if the above isn't clear.

Currently the run engine can not have more than one open run at a time so at generation time you are guaranteed never see interleaved documents if you are just watching the event stream.  DataBroker is similar (because that is the easiest way to implement it or you have a `Header` object which only has documents associated with the current Start.

There are mid-to-long term plans to allow multiple runs to be open in the run engine so the live stream will have interleaved runs.  The callbacks consuming the documents will have to be able to cope with that extra complexity.

Not passing in a gzipped vg file will be the most important thing you can
do to avoid this particular error.

(Moving the discussion about naming pairs from the stanford task here)

Making the hueristic a bit more deterministic ala the regex mentioned:
 /.+?[._-]R?([1])(.fastq(.gz)?|.fq(.gz)?)$/

sounds like a good step in the convention over configuration school of design.

I'd also like to come up with a standard way to specify inputs where the name is not depended on in any way with an eye towards automated systems to ingest, process, and forward. For example if one of two fastq pairs is sent to Treehouse by the partner or one of our systems uploading it to s3 then we should be able to kick off a toil run and point to

s3.amazonaws.com/<bucketname>/c7/96/c7964be8ab814bed8279f75e8e8a5041

as one of the pairs. Alternately if someone sends us the pairs in a tar file we'd need to be able to specify within the tar. There I could see requiring some sort of naming convention on the partners end as they'll have to specify to Treehouse somehow. Key though is to avoid having to download and tar up separate files, rename them somehow in s3, or download and untar only to re-tar...

The `request` package doesn't allow to specify what is the minimal required SSL version together with the directive to allow to use the highest SSL/TLS version available for both sides. ([requests issue 2118](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/2118))  I think that it is more secure to require explicitely the lowest version supported by SFDC than to allow an arbitrary (old broken) version that can be false reported by "man in the middle" attacker as the highest supported by the server. I implemented the restriction after the last change made by SFDC and I did set it to curent TLS v 1.0. Now we must make the TLS version configurable and set the default version to TLS 1.1 to be easy to verify readiness of deploments of django-salesforce in advance.

The standard solution of TLS 1.1 and 1.2 for Python older than 2.7.9 is by installing additional packages:

    pip install pyopenssl ndg-httpsclient pyasn1

More information can be found in `requests` package in docs string `site-packages/requests/packages/urllib3/contrib/pyopenssl.py`

Now we must check the presence of `ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1_1` and recommend these packages if the constant is not found (that means Python is old).

Ugh, I hate that I ever wrote the `geo_json` method. Terrible API design. 

Food for thought: If I Were To Rewrite This Today:
* That method would be called `data_overlay`, to follow the `image_overlay` convention.
* `data_overlay`s could be GeoJSON, TopoJSON, GeoPandas object, whatever. 
* When `overlay` is called, a `FoliumDataSource` is created. This class would be the translation layer between the various file formats and GeoJSON, which would be used to build the map. No matter what data source is backing your `FoliumDataSource`, and API to access GeoJSON from it will be the same. This is the API that `data_overlay` will interact with. 
* Any given Folium map can have as many overlays as you want. Folium should be keeping track of all overlays (image and data) via something more robust than `mark_cnt`, and the user should be able to easily remove overlays from their map.

I'm with @ebenolson here, which is why I think in the long term, having separate libraries is the way to go. The design principles underlying Lasagne, and even most of the interface choices we have made, are not closely tied to Theano. They could just as well be applied in a library built on top of TensorFlow instead (or even MXNet, CGT, ...).

I don't think this would be too much work -- for one, other people could work on it (i.e., TensorFlow users), and there would be no extra burden for either "side" of the divide to keep up with the other. They would just follow the same design principles and make similar interface choices. An added benefit would be that it would be really easy to switch from one to the other (although this would not be a primary goal, more of a side effect).

I don't think this is really "in concordance with `filter_size` and `stride`" but I guess it could be a useful feature to have. Ideally it would have to be implemented for all 4 convolution implementations though.

Just a thought: why not have the methods be asynchronous but return something like a Future which can either be ignored or waited on.

First you would get a future like so:
```python
takeoff_future = vehicle.takeoff(100)
```

Then later on, if you want to wait until the previous command succeeds (or fails):
```python
takeoff_future.wait()
if takeoff_future:
    print "Drone has reached target altitude"
else:
    print "Takeoff failed with error {}".format(takeoff_future.error)
```
Here I decided to have the truthiness of the future be indicative of an error, but you could use a property like `takeoff_future.success`.

Since you're not always waiting for a value, but sometimes for a condition to be met, you could set a callback to check for success:
```python
takeoff_future.callback = lambda: vehicle.location.alt >= desired_altitude * .95
```
Though, callbacks can be a bit unsightly in Python, so another idea is to have each function take in a future as an optional (keyword) parameter so that you can create a future specific to the function and fill in properties prior to using it.
```python
future = Future(takeoff) # pass in the function you want a future for and it will preload some defaults
future.variance = .05 # this field is unique to a takeoff future

vehicle.takeoff(100, future=future)
future.wait()
if not future:
    print future.error
```

Are you seeing monitoring commands which have been queued and not run when there are many available monitoring threads?

I'm assuming that you're using the DIRAC backend? in which case the monitoring system is supposed to queue additional commands to be run on the monitoring threads which are exposed through the 'queues' command. These commands are constructed by the monitoring loop as it finds a job which needs updating.

If there are commands which have been queued and not running then there is a bug in the Monitoring queues system.

If there are simply not many commands being queued at once then this could be due to a bottleneck in the monitoring loop code. (This ganga job monitoring code is sometimes extremely slow when first starting up and when dealing with large numbers of (sub)jobs. As I've said I'm working on optimising this.)


I hope this clears things up a bit,

I think this is simply an implementation decision. The network takes different input sizes when acting in the environment (i.e. a single observation per environment instance) versus when training (i.e. a minibatch of observations). It was probably easier to just use the same network twice with different input sizes rather than share a single dynamically-sized placeholder. But there aren't really two networks because `reuse=True` in the second declaration.

Hi all, this is the workaround/hack which I've come up in order to respect the environments (possibly asymmetric) action bounds. In the baselines/ddpg/training.py I've added a scaling of the actions before they are executed.

```
# ...
for t_rollout in range(nb_rollout_steps):
    # Predict next action.
    action, q = agent.pi(obs, apply_noise=True, compute_Q=True)
    # action is an array with entries between -1 and 1

    # scale for execution in env (as far as DDPG is concerned, every action is in [-1, 1])
    target = scale_range(action, -1, 1, env.action_space.low, env.action_space.high)
    # target is an array with scaled actions
    
    # Execute next action.
    if rank == 0 and render:
        env.render()
    assert target.shape == env.action_space.shape
    new_obs, r, done, info = env.step(target)
    t += 1
    if rank == 0 and render:
        env.render()
    # ...

def scale_range(x, x_min, x_max, y_min, y_max):
    """ Scales the entries in x which have a range between x_min and x_max
    to the range defined between y_min and y_max. """
    # y = a*x + b
    # a = deltaY/deltaX
    # b = y_min - a*x_min (or b = y_max - a*x_max)
    y = (y_max - y_min) / (x_max - x_min) * x + (y_min*x_max - y_max*x_min) / (x_max - x_min)
    return y
```

This is just a simple linear scaling from the [-1, 1] range of the DDPG algorithm to the action range provided by the environment (e.g. [-3, 22.5]). It works for multiple action dimensions as well.
I don't know if that's the way to go, but it worked for me (at least I could cope somehow with the asymmetric action bounds of my environment). If you have any concerns, feel free to comment on my solution.

Here's an simple optimization script that uses pyomo (example_opt.py):

```
from pyomo.environ import *

def opt(output_path):
    model = ConcreteModel()
    model.x = Var()
    model.constraint = Constraint(expr=0 <= model.x <= 10)
    model.objective = Objective(expr=model.x)

    # this is where the error "signal only works in main thread" happens
    result = SolverFactory("glpk").solve(model)
    xvalue = value(model.x)
    output_path1 = output_path

    return xvalue,output_path1

if __name__ == '__main__':
    output_path = 'folder'
    x,path = opt(output_path)
    print('x = ' +str(x))
    print('path = ' + str(path))
```

And here's the script that invokes it in a Thread: 

```
import os
import example_opt
from threading import Thread

def calc_test(output_path):

    thread = Thread(target=example_opt.opt,args = (output_path,))
    thread.start()
    thread.join()

if __name__ == '__main__':
  output_path = 'new_Outputs'

  if not os.path.exists(output_path):
      os.makedirs(output_path,exist_ok=True)

  calc_test(output_path)


```

Related to that (and more important) - we have also found that using tensorboard to read directly from GCS using default reload intervals incurs incredible costs. There is a cost related wth count of API calls made to access GCS and it seems that every reload directly from GCP causes every single file to be accessed separately via the GCS api . when you have bigger (but not outrageous) number of log files (few thousands is enough) It can very quickly get out of control - and with the default reload frequency of tensorboard it might get your GCS costs unreasonably high (in our case it was about 4 USD/day with having 3000 files in the logs !!!). We got about 4 millions of API requests generated in 4 days (!) just by running one tensorboard instance. We are changing strategy now to sync the GCS data locally (using gsutil rsync) and read it from there because of that.

From the timestamp logs it looks like large chunk transfers exhibit several hundred ms pauses on the receiving end _between transfers_, which slows down the transfer. In all cases there are also 1us delays between each each chunk read/write, which is 20-30% overhead in the event loop when using small chunks. I think these event loop delays alone can explain the overhead -- small chunks have lots of 1us delays, and large chunks cause large delays.

I would look into why the event loop is behaving this way; probably it can be debugged with more prints or something. Particularly the 1us delays between write ops, that's quite suspicious. Maybe there's some mis-configuration of the event loop to sleep 1us?

@mehrdadn thanks for the review! I think this discussion makes it clear that there is a ton of work to do to convert the project to a C++ style #406. It's probably not desirable to radically change the style locally (in this PR) without doing it more globally as well. That is, if we're using malloc/free for memory management everywhere else in the project, and we need to use something in the C++ STL, then it looks like the simplest thing to do is to do a direct translation from malloc/free to new/delete and to defer the usage of unique_ptr a little further down the road when we do a project-wide change.

As we discussed earlier, one elegant approach is to provide a single method for running a function on all workers, and pass a counter into that function indicating how many other workers on that machine have already started executing that function (using Redis to do this atomically).

So if there are 4 worker on one machine, and we call something like `ray.run_function_on_all_workers(f)`, then one worker will call `f(1)`, one worker will call `f(2)`, one will call `f(3)`, and one will call `f(4)`.

A couple of remarks on the downloaded Matroska files:

1. Most Hardware players and Smart TVs currently do not support the Opus audio decoder; 
4K downloaded files are better viewed on 4K big TV monitors rather than desktops - 
and few (if any) laptop monitors have UHD capabilities, certainly not older ones... 
So, IMHO, there should be a setting (like perhaps in youtube-dl) that would allow 
for the AAC dash audio stream to be muxed with the h264 (AVC) video one.

2. As the FFmpeg and MediaInfo logs show, the created Matroska container 
has issues; no file bitrate (N/A), hence no actual duration length (N/A) can be 
calculated from container data; it is up to the software player to deal with that...

I have resolved those container inconsistencies by remuxing them with mkvmerge 
(part of mkvtoolnix); new MediaInfo reports follow: 
[YT-4320p-RMX.mkv.txt](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/files/619818/YT-4320p-RMX.mkv.txt)
[YT-2160p-RMX.mkv.txt](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/files/619819/YT-2160p-RMX.mkv.txt)
As you can see, the 4320p labeled file (in reality 2160p) has a lower bitrate (15.7 Mbps) 
than the 2160p labeled one (18.4 Mbps), hence the smaller file size.

If you need more tests with a truly 4320p stream, just point me to one and I will 
report back when time permits...

Regards

First steps (probably 7.1 release)
 * widgets will have a nosync attribute on the js side, so that these backbone attributes will not be send over the wire.
 * selection widgets (on the js side), will set the 'label' attribute when index change (and also the inverse when label is set) but this attribute will not be synced to the backend using the new nosync attributes.

```python
from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String, ForeignKey, UniqueConstraint, func, DateTime
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship


Base = declarative_base()


class CommonColumns(Base):
    __abstract__ = True
    _created = Column(DateTime, default=func.now())
    _updated = Column(DateTime, default=func.now(), onupdate=func.now())
    _etag = Column(String(40))


class DevicesServices(CommonColumns):
    __tablename__ ='devices2services'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    service_id =  Column(Integer, ForeignKey('services.service_id'), nullable=False)
    device_mac = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('devices.device_mac'), nullable=False)
    UniqueConstraint('service_id', 'device_id', name='ServiceDeviceUnique')
    local_port = Column(Integer)
    pid = Column(Integer)
    services = relationship("Services", back_populates="service_devices")
    devices = relationship("Devices", back_populates="device_services")


class Devices(CommonColumns):
    __tablename__ = 'devices'
    asset_tag = Column(String(127))
    device_hostname = Column(String(255))
    device_mac = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    device_ip = Column(String(24))
    device_services = relationship("DevicesServices", back_populates="devices")
    zone_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('zones.zone_id'))
    device_zone = relationship("Zones", back_populates="zone_devices")


class Services(CommonColumns):
    __tablename__ = 'services'
    service_id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    service_name = Column(String(255))
    service_port = Column(Integer())
    service_devices = relationship("DevicesServices", back_populates="services")


class Zones(CommonColumns):
    __tablename__ = 'zones'
    zone_id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    zone_name = Column(String(255))
    zone_devices = relationship("Devices", back_populates="device_zone")
```

> I tried it without sudo and the jobs failed when trying to download 3.7 for 14.04 rather than 16.04 (which was running).

Okay, I dug into this some more and here's what I think is going on. [Let's look at this big table of their supported build environments.](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/reference/overview/)

The first thing we notice is that 16.04 xenial isn't mentioned *at all*. Obviously they are working on it behind the scenes, given that they have Python builds available for it and everything, but using xenial is not actually a documented/supported option yet. We also see that they have two main platforms they run on – virtual machines on GCE, and container images on EC2. It looks like `sudo: required` is how you request to be routed to GCE, and also that GCE is the only platform where they support multiple operating systems. So my guess is that *if* we don't set `sudo: required`, then the `dist: xenial` part is simply ignored, and we're running on trusty in a container on EC2.

And in fact i fyou look at the [log that we got](https://travis-ci.org/RazerM/outcome/jobs/400606832), and click on the little arrow to expand "Worker information" we see that it's running on ec2, and if we click to expand "Build system information" it says "Build dist: xenial" which sounds good but then you look down a little further and find that under "Operating system description" it says we're actually running on 14.04 trusty.

And 3.7, 3.8-dev aren't supported on trusty, because it uses an ancient version of openssl – this part *is* documented on their python page, and can also be confirmed by checking the s3 bucket directly.

OKAY SO what does this mean for us. Obviously we have to use `sudo: required; dist: xenial` for our 3.7 and 3.8-dev builds, because that's the only thing that works.

There's no point in using `dist: xenial` *without* `sudo: required`, because that actually ends up running on trusty and it's just going to confuse us later. (Or possibly break stuff if they ever do roll out support for xenial on their container infrastructure.)

We want to avoid using `sudo: required` whenever possible, because if we consult that table again we see that (a) starting jobs is substantially slower (boot time: "20-50s" vs "1-6s"), and (b) IPv6 is disabled so all our IPv6 tests can't run.

CONCLUSION: For now I think we want to use `dist: xenial, sudo: required` for only the 3.7 and 3.8 jobs where we have no choice, and for all the other jobs we should stick with `dist: trusty`.

I also found that setting bucket notification configuration does not work through java sdk.
From what I can see, `awscli` sends PUT request to `http://localhost:4572/<bucket-name>?notification`, while `AmazonS3` in AWS SDK will send the notification to `http://<bucket-name>.<endopoint>/?notification`. This means that configuration done through java sdk does not get applied.
